activities_projects.title: Comparative Analysis: Constitutional vs. Universal Rights
activities_projects.objective: Critically evaluate the gap between revolutionary rhetoric and legislative reality
steps: Analyze the 'Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen' [cite: 345] alongside the specific voting qualifications of the 1791 Constitution[cite: 311].
Explain the paradox of a document claiming 'Men are born and remain free and equal' [cite: 346] while establishing a system of 'Passive Citizens' without voting rights[cite: 312].
Incorporate Jean-Paul Marat’s critique from 'L’Ami du peuple' regarding how wealth influences the law[cite: 337, 339].
Write a 500-word essay on whether the 1791 Constitution fulfilled the promise of the Revolution.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Develops critical thinking regarding the contradictions in early democratic frameworks
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of argument and effective use of contrasting sources (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Sociopolitical Simulation: The Estates General Deadlock
activities_projects.objective: Understand the procedural mechanics of the transition from Feudalism to a National Assembly
steps: Divide the class into three estates with the correct historical ratios (300:300:600)[cite: 194].
Simulate the 5 May 1789 meeting where the First and Second Estates are seated and the Third Estate stands at the back[cite: 194].
Debate the transition from 'estate-based voting' (one vote per estate) to 'head-based voting' (one vote per member)[cite: 198, 200].
Draft the 'Tennis Court Oath' collectively, justifying the decision to declare as a National Assembly[cite: 205].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the shift from feudal privilege to national sovereignty
activities_projects.evaluation: Quality of debate and historical accuracy in the drafted oath (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Economic Historiography: The Subsistence Crisis
activities_projects.objective: Synthesize demographic, climatic, and economic data to explain social collapse
steps: Map the population growth from 23 million in 1715 to 28 million in 1789 and its impact on food demand[cite: 122].
Analyze how the fixity of wages against the rising price of bread widened the rich-poor gap[cite: 125, 126, 127].
Complete the flow of a 'Subsistence Crisis' in Fig. 4 by inserting terms like 'Food riots' and 'increased number of deaths'[cite: 138, 153].
Research how 'hail or drought' acted as the final catalyst for the 1789 outbreak[cite: 127].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the multi-dimensional causes of revolutionary volatility
activities_projects.evaluation: Technical accuracy in completing the economic flow and data analysis (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Gender and Political Theory: The Gouges vs. Chaumette Debate
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the philosophical justifications for and against gender equality in 1793
steps: Deconstruct Olympe de Gouges’ claim that 'Sovereignty resides in the nation, which is nothing but the union of woman and man'[cite: 567].
Contrast this with Chaumette’s biological essentialism argument about 'domestic duties' and 'motherhood'[cite: 582, 588].
Explain the irony of the Jacobin government using the law to exclude women from the very 'General Will' they claimed to represent[cite: 544, 568].
Predict the impact of these closures on the women's movement over the next 200 years[cite: 546].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Examines the limits of Enlightenment thinking regarding gender
activities_projects.evaluation: Critical analysis of the two conflicting worldviews (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Global Paradox: Slavery and the Rights of Man
activities_projects.objective: Examine the tension between colonial mercantilism and revolutionary ideals
steps: Trace the 'Triangular Slave Trade' and explain why port cities like Bordeaux and Nantes flourished[cite: 598, 602].
Analyze why the National Assembly feared passing laws to abolish slavery (influence of businessmen)[cite: 605].
Compare the 1794 Convention decree freeing slaves with Napoleon’s 1804 reintroduction of slavery[cite: 606, 607].
Interpret the 1794 print (Fig. 14) and its depiction of the 'civilizing' mission[cite: 611, 613].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Connects economic interests to the delay of moral and human rights reforms
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity in explaining the legislative timeline and image interpretation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Political Symbolism: An Etching Interpretation
activities_projects.objective: Synthesize visual art and social commentary using the 'Spider and the Fly' metaphor
steps: Interpret the etching 'The Spider and the Fly' (Fig. 3) where the nobleman is the spider and the peasant is the fly[cite: 118, 117].
Explain the meaning of the caption 'The more the devil has, the more he wants' in the context of feudal dues[cite: 121, 79].
Link the visual of the peasant bringing 'grain, fruits, money' to the specific taxes of Tithes and Taille[cite: 107, 81, 82].
Create an original modern-day metaphor for social inequality based on this historical etching.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Translates 18th-century visual propaganda into socio-economic theory
activities_projects.evaluation: Creativity and depth of historical connection (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Jacobin Ideology: Terror as 'Justice'
activities_projects.objective: Critically analyze the moral justification for state violence
steps: Analyze Robespierre's definition of Terror as 'justice, swift, severe and inflexible'[cite: 488].
Contrast this with Camille Desmoulins' warning that guillotining enemies only creates ten more enemies[cite: 484].
List the cultural changes forced by the Jacobins, such as 'equality bread' and the replacement of 'Monsieur/Madame' with 'Citoyen/Citoyenne'[cite: 466, 468].
Debate: Is state violence ever justifiable to 'protect' democracy? Use the text to support both sides[cite: 487, 490].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the ethical dilemmas of revolutionary governance
activities_projects.evaluation: Logic and evidence-based reasoning in the debate (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Napoleonic Duality: Liberator vs. Invader
activities_projects.objective: Assess Napoleon’s role as both a destroyer of dynasties and a modernizer
steps: Identify Napoleon’s 'modernizing' laws: protection of private property and the decimal system[cite: 651].
Explain the transition in European sentiment from seeing him as a 'liberator' to an 'invading force'[cite: 652, 653].
Evaluate his defeat at Waterloo (1815) and why his ideas survived his political fall[cite: 654].
Discuss how his rise was paved by the 'political instability' of the Directory[cite: 505].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes the complex legacy of military dictatorship in political evolution
activities_projects.evaluation: Comprehensiveness of the duality analysis (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The 'Great Fear' and Rural Dynamics
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the spread of revolution through rumor and panic
steps: Describe the 'frenzy of fear' caused by rumors of 'bands of brigands' hired by lords[cite: 237, 238].
Explain the significance of peasants burning 'manorial dues' documents[cite: 239].
Analyze why the night of 4 August 1789 (abolishing the feudal system) was a direct result of this rural violence[cite: 241, 242].
Map the spread of the 'Great Fear' using Fig. 6 and identify the regions most affected[cite: 275, 277].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the symbiotic relationship between urban politics and rural revolt
activities_projects.evaluation: Map accuracy and analysis of the 4 August decree (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Historiographical Legacy: The French Revolution and India
activities_projects.objective: Connect European revolutionary events to global anti-colonial movements
steps: Investigate how Tipu Sultan and Rammohan Roy 'responded to the ideas coming from revolutionary France'[cite: 664].
Analyze Raja Rammohan Roy’s specific excitement regarding the July Revolution of 1830[cite: 668, 669].
Explain the concept of 'reworking' revolutionary ideas into a 'language of politics' suitable for colonised nations[cite: 15, 663].
Identify five democratic rights in the modern Indian Constitution that find their roots in 1789[cite: 684].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Appreciates the global cross-pollination of political ideals
activities_projects.evaluation: Ability to draw logical connections between French and Indian historical contexts (10 marks)
